verses.text: And Satan stood up against Israel, and provoked David to number Israel. And David said to Joab and to the rulers of the people, Go, number Israel from Beersheba even to Dan; and bring the number of them to me, that I may know it. And Joab answered, The LORD make his people an hundred times so many more as they be: but, my lord the king, are they not all my lord's servants? why then doth my lord require this thing? why will he be a cause of trespass to Israel? Nevertheless the king's word prevailed against Joab. Wherefore Joab departed, and went throughout all Israel, and came to Jerusalem. And Joab gave the sum of the number of the people unto David. And all they of Israel were a thousand thousand and an hundred thousand men that drew sword: and Judah was four hundred threescore and ten thousand men that drew sword. But Levi and Benjamin counted he not among them: for the king's word was abominable to Joab. Guidance: A dark turn comes in David's reign: 'Satan stood up against Israel, and provoked David to number Israel.' At first glance the census seems harmless — but it sprang from pride and a desire to glory in the strength of his armies rather than in the LORD his God. Even Joab, no tender conscience, saw the evil and protested, 'why will he be a cause of trespass to Israel?' Yet the king's word prevailed, and the sinful count was taken. The chapter is showing us how sin enters even the heart of a godly man — through the temptation of the adversary working on pride. David, who had so often trusted God against giants and armies, now wanted to measure his might by numbers, to lean on the size of his host. The chapter is teaching us the subtlety of pride: it can taint what looks like a reasonable act, turning a census into a trespass because the heart behind it sought confidence in flesh rather than in God. The chapter is teaching us that Satan is ever 'standing up against' God's people, seeking to provoke us to the sins that flatter our self-reliance. The chapter is teaching us, too, the value of godly counsel and the danger of overriding it: Joab, of all people, warned the king, yet David's word prevailed, and he plunged ahead. When even a Joab counsels caution and we will not hear, we are in peril. The chapter is teaching us to beware of trusting in our resources, our numbers, our visible strength — for the LORD will not share His glory, and the heart that counts its armies to find security has begun to forsake its God. Let us number our mercies, not our might, and put our trust in the living God alone.

And God was displeased with this thing; therefore he smote Israel. And David said unto God, I have sinned greatly, because I have done this thing: but now, I beseech thee, do away the iniquity of thy servant; for I have done very foolishly. And the LORD spake unto Gad, David's seer, saying, Go and tell David, saying, Thus saith the LORD, I offer thee three things: choose thee one of them, that I may do it unto thee. So Gad came to David, and said unto him, Thus saith the LORD, Choose thee Either three years' famine; or three months to be destroyed before thy foes, while that the sword of thine enemies overtaketh thee; or else three days the sword of the LORD, even the pestilence, in the land, and the angel of the LORD destroying throughout all the coasts of Israel. Now therefore advise thyself what word I shall bring again to him that sent me. And David said unto Gad, I am in a great strait: let me fall now into the hand of the LORD; for very great are his mercies: but let me not fall into the hand of man. Guidance: God is displeased, and David, awakened to his sin, makes a true and humble confession: 'I have sinned greatly... do away the iniquity of thy servant; for I have done very foolishly.' No excuses, no blaming Joab or Satan — David owns it fully. Then God, through Gad the seer, offers him a choice of three judgments, and David's reply is one of the most beautiful confessions of faith in all of Scripture: 'let me fall now into the hand of the LORD; for very great are his mercies: but let me not fall into the hand of man.' The chapter is showing us the marks of genuine repentance: David freely confesses, calls his sin great and foolish, and casts himself wholly on the mercy of God. The chapter is teaching us how to confess — plainly, without self-justification, owning both the guilt ('I have sinned greatly') and the folly ('I have done very foolishly') of our sin. The chapter is teaching us, supremely, the refuge of the penitent heart: when David must choose how to be chastened, he chooses to fall into the hand of God rather than man, 'for very great are his mercies.' Here is faith reasoning rightly even under the rod: God's hand, even in judgment, is more merciful than the hand of men; better to be corrected by a loving Father than spared by a cruel world. The chapter is teaching us that the mercies of the LORD are 'very great,' so great that we may flee to Him even when He is chastening us — for the same God who wounds is the God who heals, and His severest dealings are tempered with compassion. When we have sinned and judgment looms, let us do as David did: confess fully, and throw ourselves upon the great mercies of God, who will not break the bruised reed nor cast off the penitent who comes to Him.

No excuses, no blaming Joab or Satan — David owns it fully. Then God, through Gad the seer, offers him a choice of three judgments, and David's reply is one of the most beautiful confessions of faith in all of Scripture: 'let me fall now into the hand of the LORD; for very great are his mercies: but let me not fall into the hand of man.' The chapter is showing us the marks of genuine repentance: David freely confesses, calls his sin great and foolish, and casts himself wholly on the mercy of God. The chapter is teaching us how to confess — plainly, without self-justification, owning both the guilt ('I have sinned greatly') and the folly ('I have done very foolishly') of our sin. The chapter is teaching us, supremely, the refuge of the penitent heart: when David must choose how to be chastened, he chooses to fall into the hand of God rather than man, 'for very great are his mercies.' Here is faith reasoning rightly even under the rod: God's hand, even in judgment, is more merciful than the hand of men; better to be corrected by a loving Father than spared by a cruel world. The chapter is teaching us that the mercies of the LORD are 'very great,' so great that we may flee to Him even when He is chastening us — for the same God who wounds is the God who heals, and His severest dealings are tempered with compassion. When we have sinned and judgment looms, let us do as David did: confess fully, and throw ourselves upon the great mercies of God, who will not break the bruised reed nor cast off the penitent who comes to Him.

So the LORD sent pestilence upon Israel: and there fell of Israel seventy thousand men. And God sent an angel unto Jerusalem to destroy it: and as he was destroying, the LORD beheld, and he repented him of the evil, and said to the angel that destroyed, It is enough, stay now thine hand. And the angel of the LORD stood by the threshingfloor of Ornan the Jebusite. And David lifted up his eyes, and saw the angel of the LORD stand between the earth and the heaven, having a drawn sword in his hand stretched out over Jerusalem. Then David and the elders of Israel, who were clothed in sackcloth, fell upon their faces. And David said unto God, Is it not I that commanded the people to be numbered? even I it is that have sinned and done evil indeed; but as for these sheep, what have they done? let thine hand, I pray thee, O LORD my God, be on me, and on my father's house; but not on thy people, that they should be plagued. Guidance: The plague falls and seventy thousand die; then, as the destroying angel stretches his sword over Jerusalem, 'the LORD beheld, and he repented him of the evil, and said... It is enough, stay now thine hand.' And David, seeing the angel with drawn sword, falls on his face and cries out one of the noblest intercessions in all the Bible: 'Is it not I that commanded the people to be numbered?... but as for these sheep, what have they done? let thine hand, I pray thee, O LORD my God, be on me... but not on thy people.' The chapter is showing us the LORD's mercy that sets a limit to judgment — 'It is enough' — for He does not afflict willingly, and His compassions fail not. The chapter is teaching us that even in righteous judgment God remembers mercy; He stayed the angel's hand before Jerusalem was destroyed. And the chapter is teaching us the heart of a true shepherd-king in David's plea: he takes the whole blame upon himself ('it is I that have sinned'), pleads for his people as innocent sheep, and offers himself and his house to bear the stroke in their stead — 'let thine hand be on me... but not on thy people.' Here the shepherd offers to die for the sheep. The chapter is teaching us the beauty of intercession and self-sacrificing love: David would rather suffer himself than see his people perish for his sin. And in this we see, dim but real, a foreshadowing of the great Shepherd-King, great David's Son, who took the whole guilt of His people upon Himself — though He, unlike David, was sinless — and bore in His own body the sword of judgment that we, the guilty sheep, might be spared. The chapter is teaching us to plead for others, to take responsibility for our sin, and to marvel at the Shepherd who truly laid down His life for the sheep.

Then the angel of the LORD commanded Gad to say to David, that David should go up, and set up an altar unto the LORD in the threshingfloor of Ornan the Jebusite. And David went up at the saying of Gad, which he spake in the name of the LORD. And Ornan turned back, and saw the angel; and his four sons with him hid themselves. Now Ornan was threshing wheat. And as David came to Ornan, Ornan looked and saw David, and went out of the threshingfloor, and bowed himself to David with his face to the ground. Then David said to Ornan, Grant me the place of this threshingfloor, that I may build an altar therein unto the LORD: thou shalt grant it me for the full price: that the plague may be stayed from the people. And Ornan said unto David, Take it to thee, and let my lord the king do that which is good in his eyes: lo, I give thee the oxen also for burnt offerings, and the threshing instruments for wood, and the wheat for the meat offering; I give it all. And king David said to Ornan, Nay; but I will verily buy it for the full price: for I will not take that which is thine for the LORD, nor offer burnt offerings without cost. So David gave to Ornan for the place six hundred shekels of gold by weight. And David built there an altar unto the LORD, and offered burnt offerings and peace offerings, and called upon the LORD; and he answered him from heaven by fire upon the altar of burnt offering. Guidance: At God's command David goes to build an altar on the threshingfloor of Ornan, where the angel had stood. Ornan generously offers to give the place, the oxen, and the wood for nothing — but David refuses with words that ring through the ages: 'Nay; but I will verily buy it for the full price: for I will not take that which is thine for the LORD, nor offer burnt offerings without cost.' He pays six hundred shekels of gold, builds the altar, offers sacrifice, and God answers 'from heaven by fire upon the altar' — the sign of acceptance and of the plague's end. The chapter is showing us the principle of costly worship: David would not give God an offering that cost him nothing. The chapter is teaching us that true devotion to God must be sacrificial; a religion that costs us nothing is worth nothing, and to offer the LORD what we got for free, sparing our own purse and our own ease, is to dishonour Him. David teaches us to bring God our best at real cost to ourselves. The chapter is teaching us, in the fire from heaven, that God accepts the sacrifice of the penitent and answers the cry of the contrite; the same place where the angel's sword was drawn becomes the place where atonement is made and judgment is stayed. The chapter is teaching us the deep gospel pattern: at the threshingfloor, the sword of judgment is sheathed because a sacrifice has been offered and accepted by fire — pointing forward to the cross, where the full price was paid (not by us, but by Christ), and the fire of God's wrath was satisfied, so that the plague of sin might be stayed from all who believe. This very threshingfloor, bought at full price and hallowed by fire, would become the site of the temple itself — the place of Israel's worship was born from a place of judgment turned to mercy through sacrifice. The chapter is teaching us to worship God at cost, to trust the sacrifice He has provided, and to see in this altar the shadow of the cross where atonement was made forever.

And as David came to Ornan, Ornan looked and saw David, and went out of the threshingfloor, and bowed himself to David with his face to the ground. Then David said to Ornan, Grant me the place of this threshingfloor, that I may build an altar therein unto the LORD: thou shalt grant it me for the full price: that the plague may be stayed from the people. And Ornan said unto David, Take it to thee, and let my lord the king do that which is good in his eyes: lo, I give thee the oxen also for burnt offerings, and the threshing instruments for wood, and the wheat for the meat offering; I give it all. And king David said to Ornan, Nay; but I will verily buy it for the full price: for I will not take that which is thine for the LORD, nor offer burnt offerings without cost. So David gave to Ornan for the place six hundred shekels of gold by weight. And David built there an altar unto the LORD, and offered burnt offerings and peace offerings, and called upon the LORD; and he answered him from heaven by fire upon the altar of burnt offering. Guidance: At God's command David goes to build an altar on the threshingfloor of Ornan, where the angel had stood. Ornan generously offers to give the place, the oxen, and the wood for nothing — but David refuses with words that ring through the ages: 'Nay; but I will verily buy it for the full price: for I will not take that which is thine for the LORD, nor offer burnt offerings without cost.' He pays six hundred shekels of gold, builds the altar, offers sacrifice, and God answers 'from heaven by fire upon the altar' — the sign of acceptance and of the plague's end. The chapter is showing us the principle of costly worship: David would not give God an offering that cost him nothing. The chapter is teaching us that true devotion to God must be sacrificial; a religion that costs us nothing is worth nothing, and to offer the LORD what we got for free, sparing our own purse and our own ease, is to dishonour Him. David teaches us to bring God our best at real cost to ourselves. The chapter is teaching us, in the fire from heaven, that God accepts the sacrifice of the penitent and answers the cry of the contrite; the same place where the angel's sword was drawn becomes the place where atonement is made and judgment is stayed. The chapter is teaching us the deep gospel pattern: at the threshingfloor, the sword of judgment is sheathed because a sacrifice has been offered and accepted by fire — pointing forward to the cross, where the full price was paid (not by us, but by Christ), and the fire of God's wrath was satisfied, so that the plague of sin might be stayed from all who believe. This very threshingfloor, bought at full price and hallowed by fire, would become the site of the temple itself — the place of Israel's worship was born from a place of judgment turned to mercy through sacrifice. The chapter is teaching us to worship God at cost, to trust the sacrifice He has provided, and to see in this altar the shadow of the cross where atonement was made forever.

And the LORD commanded the angel; and he put up his sword again into the sheath thereof. At that time when David saw that the LORD had answered him in the threshingfloor of Ornan the Jebusite, then he sacrificed there. For the tabernacle of the LORD, which Moses made in the wilderness, and the altar of the burnt offering, were at that season in the high place at Gibeon. But David could not go before it to enquire of God: for he was afraid because of the sword of the angel of the LORD. Guidance: The judgment ends: 'the LORD commanded the angel; and he put up his sword again into the sheath thereof.' The wrath is stayed, the sacrifice accepted, the people spared. The Chronicler notes that the old tabernacle and altar were still at Gibeon, but David, awed and afraid 'because of the sword of the angel,' worshiped at this new altar where God had so visibly answered him. The chapter is showing us the blessed end of repentance and sacrifice: the sword of judgment returns to its sheath. When sin is confessed, the sacrifice offered, and mercy sought, God stays His hand — the threatening sword is put away. The chapter is teaching us that there is forgiveness with God, that His judgments have an appointed end for the penitent, and that the contrite who flee to His mercy find the sword sheathed and peace restored. The chapter is teaching us, too, a holy and reverent fear of God: David was 'afraid because of the sword of the angel' — having seen the awful holiness and power of the LORD, he was filled with reverence. Such fear is not the terror that drives us from God but the awe that bows us before Him; David had seen how dreadful sin is and how mighty the LORD, and it humbled him. The chapter is teaching us that the place where God answers by fire becomes the place of true worship; David recognized that here, at Ornan's threshingfloor, God had met him in mercy, and so here he worshiped. The chapter is preparing us for what comes next — for this very spot, where the sword was sheathed and the fire fell, would be chosen as the site of the temple, the house of God for all Israel. Out of judgment and mercy, repentance and sacrifice, God brought forth the place of His dwelling. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD turns our places of chastening into places of worship, and meets His humbled people with mercy where they feared only wrath.
